title: Scientists’ Side Hustle? Using AI and Quantum Computing to Generate New Peptides
gist: A Technical University of Denmark team, self-funded on weekends, paired a generative AI drug-discovery model with a printer-sized quantum computer from British startup ORCA Computing and produced more successful novel peptides than a classical AI alone, showing the biggest gains where training data was scarce — an early but limited proof point for near-term commercial quantum-AI hybrid applications.

insights.claim: A DTU (Technical University of Denmark) team combined a generative AI protein-prediction model with a printer-sized quantum computer built by British startup ORCA Computing to generate novel peptides capable of binding to specific proteins, a key step in vaccine development.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a working hybrid quantum-classical AI pipeline applied to a real drug-discovery task, an early commercial proof point for quantum computing vendors and biotech AI tool developers.
insights.locator: The Technical University of Denmark team ran their generative AI model

insights.claim: Laboratory testing showed the quantum-hybrid model produced more successful peptides (ones that actually bound to target proteins) than its classical AI counterpart, with the strongest improvements occurring where training data was rare.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Quantum advantage appearing specifically in low-data regimes suggests a targeted, near-term use case (rare diseases, understudied populations) rather than a general performance edge — important for calibrating quantum-AI investment expectations.
insights.locator: Making the peptides in the laboratory and testing

insights.claim: The research team funded the project by working weekends and pooling unspent money left over from other projects, because, according to lead researcher Timothy Patrick Jenkins, 'most innovative science is too scary for foundations.'
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Highlights a funding gap for exploratory, high-risk science that formal grant structures avoid, suggesting under-the-radar quantum-AI research may be more advanced than institutional funding data implies.
insights.quote: most innovative science is too scary for foundations
insights.locator: because "most innovative science is too scary for foundations,"

insights.claim: ORCA Computing CEO Richard Murray says this study is novel because it demonstrates a near-term commercial application for quantum computing, an area that has lacked clear near-term examples of usefulness.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: A quantum-computing vendor CEO directly framing this as proof of near-term commercial viability is a signal worth tracking for quantum computing investment narratives, though it comes from an interested party.
insights.quote: I think it’s no surprise that lots of industrial companies think quantum is hazy and far away
insights.locator: He says this study is novel in that it shows

insights.claim: ORCA Computing is also applying its quantum technology through separate projects with oil major BP on chemistry problems and automaker Toyota on making its design process more efficient.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Names concrete enterprise customers (BP, Toyota) beyond academia, indicating ORCA is pursuing multiple industrial verticals for quantum-AI hybrid applications, relevant for tracking quantum computing commercialization.
insights.locator: His company is also applying the technology through projects

insights.claim: Lead researcher Timothy Patrick Jenkins was initially a 'huge quantum skeptic' who believed practical application to his work was decades away, before the DTU-ORCA collaboration changed his view.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: A converted skeptic's account adds credibility to the claim of near-term quantum usefulness, though it remains a single-lab anecdote rather than broad validation.
insights.quote: I was a huge quantum skeptic
insights.locator: Even Patrick Jenkins was initially reluctant

insights.claim: DTU PhD student Jonathan Funk says current quantum computers are still too small to run full-scale, cutting-edge AI models, meaning the peptide targets tested were smaller than the normal-sized antibodies the team usually works with, and better results could still be achieved on a classical computer alone.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: A candid limitation from within the research team tempers the commercial-hype narrative, indicating quantum's practical scale-up remains a genuine bottleneck, not just a maturity curve.
insights.quote: Quantum is still not very powerful, so the level of complexity that we could encode wasn’t a normal-sized antibody, which is what we usually work with
insights.locator: says DTU PhD student Jonathan Funk

insights.claim: Jenkins' team, largely funded by the Novo Nordisk Foundation, says a major challenge is the lack of genetic data diversity across the human population since most medical research has focused on Western populations, making it hard to develop peptides effective for understudied groups in Asia and Africa.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural data bias in global drug discovery that quantum-AI methods may help offset specifically because they perform best in low-data regimes, a differentiated value proposition beyond raw speed.
insights.locator: a particular challenge for his team has been the lack of data

insights.claim: The team hypothesized that embedding a quantum computer into their AI workflow could generate a more diverse set of peptides for targets with less data, after observing quantum computers have a similar diversifying effect in image generation.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Draws an explicit analogy from quantum-enhanced generative image models to biological sequence generation, a transferable framework for where quantum-AI hybrids may add value elsewhere.
insights.locator: His team hypothesized that embedding a quantum computer

insights.claim: Jenkins' team plans next to test the quantum-AI workflow with more cutting-edge models and larger proteins, and is separately exploring using a quantum computer to enhance generative AI design of synthetic antidotes for snakebite venom.
insights.speaker: Isabella Ward
insights.so_what: Signals a concrete research roadmap and a second target application (snakebite antidotes) worth tracking as an early-stage quantum-biotech pipeline.
insights.locator: He’s also looking at using a quantum computer
